title: Destructive interference of second harmonic generation in AA stacked MoTe$_2$/WSe$_2$
authors: Yiduo Wang, Yao Lu, Changshen Chen, Xiaotong Liao, Siyu Fan, Zhenyu Wang, Yaotian Liu, Subi Du, Yingze Jia, Ye Zhu, Yingwei Wang, Jun He, Song Liu, Jiawei Ruan, Zhen Chen, Kai-Qiang Lin, Yang Xu
abstract: The stacking configuration of two-dimensional materials critically governs their optical and electronic responses. Monolayer transition-metal dichalcogenides (TMDC) lack inversion symmetry and exhibit exciton-enhanced second-harmonic generation (SHG). In TMDC bilayers, 60° (0°) stacking is conventionally expected to suppress (enhance) SHG owing to destructive (constructive) interference of the layer-resolved nonlinear polarizations. Here, we report an unconventional destructive SHG interference in nearly 0°-stacked (AA-stacked) MoTe2/WSe2 heterobilayers using two independent probes: atomic-resolution imaging and stacking-sensitive exciton hybridization measurements. Supported by ab initio GW and Bethe-Salpeter equation calculations, we show that distinct two-photon resonances associated with the WSe2 C exciton and the MoTe2 D exciton generate a nearly $π$ phase difference ($Δφ$) in their second-order nonlinear susceptibilities $χ^{(2)}$, leading to the anomalous destructive interference. We further demonstrate that in small-angle twisted MoTe2/WSe2, the SHG polarization state is governed by the interplay between twist angle $α$ and phase difference $Δφ$, and can be mapped onto trajectories on the Poincaré sphere. At excitation energies satisfying $Δφ$ + 3$α$ = 180°, the SHG output becomes nearly circularly polarized (ellipticity ~ 0.91) and undergoes an abrupt 90° azimuthal rotation, corresponding to a geometric polarization singularity in the parameter space. Our findings open new routes for exciton-resonance engineered nonlinear photonics and stacking-resolved optical functionality in moiré materials.
summary: This paper identifies that AA-stacked MoTe2/WSe2 heterobilayers exhibit unexpected destructive SHG interference caused by a phase difference between layer-specific exciton resonances. By tuning the twist angle and excitation energy, the researchers demonstrate precise control over the SHG polarization state, including the achievement of near-circular polarization. This work provides a new framework for engineering nonlinear optical functions in moiré materials.
main_problem: The paper aims to resolve the ambiguity in stacking identification (AA vs. AB) in MoTe2/WSe2 heterobilayers and understand the origin of anomalous destructive Second Harmonic Generation (SHG) interference in AA-stacked configurations.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that AA-stacked MoTe2/WSe2 exhibits unconventional destructive SHG interference due to a pi phase difference in nonlinear susceptibility driven by distinct exciton resonances, and show that the SHG polarization can be controlled via twist angle and excitation energy.
method: The study combines experimental techniques like Multislice Electron Ptychography (MEP) for atomic-resolution imaging, angle-resolved SHG spectroscopy, and reflectance contrast with theoretical GW-BSE calculations and a coherent superposition model.
model_or_system: The system consists of MoTe2/WSe2 transition-metal dichalcogenide (TMDC) heterobilayers in AA and AB stacking configurations, encapsulated in hexagonal boron nitride (hBN). The optical response is modeled using a coherent superposition of electric fields from individual layers and a two-level Hamiltonian for exciton hybridization.
key_observables: SHG intensity, polarization ellipticity, polarization azimuth, Stokes parameters, MEP phase images, and exciton hybridization (energy anticrossing).
important_parameters: Stacking configuration (AA vs. AB), twist angle (alpha), interlayer phase difference (delta-phi), excitation photon energy, and vertical electric field (E).
main_assumptions: The SHG susceptibility of the heterostructure is modeled as a linear combination of individual monolayer susceptibilities; a rigid redshift of the WSe2 spectrum is applied to account for stacking effects in calculations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows device fabrication and optical micrographs; Figure 2/3 details the SHG intensity, polarization states, and comparison between experimental and calculated dielectric functions; Supplemental figures include simulated electron microscopy images and polarization-dependent SHG models.
paper_structure: The paper begins with device fabrication and stacking identification via electron ptychography, moves to the investigation of anomalous SHG interference and its excitonic origin using spectroscopy and GW-BSE calculations, and concludes with the demonstration of polarization control and mapping of polarization trajectories on the Poincaré sphere.
why_interesting: The discovery of a geometric polarization singularity and the ability to engineer nonlinear polarization states via interlayer phase engineering is highly relevant to the field of nonlinear quantum optics and the development of tunable photonic components.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Identifying AA and AB stacking of MoTe2/WSe2 by multislice electron ptychography (MEP). (a) Optical micrograph of two isolated monolayer WSe2 flakes (outlined in red and blue) with identical crystal orientation, which are used to construct the heterostructure in (b). These flakes are sequentially picked up with a relative twist angle of 60° and stacked in a non-overlapping configuration. A subsequent MoTe2 flake is aligned and overlapped with the two WSe2 flakes using angle-resolved SHG before final assembly. (b), Optical micrograph of fully assembled device D1, encapsulated between top and bottom h-BN flakes and released onto a SiNx membrane with 2 μm holes for MEP measurements....

FIG. 2. Exciton hybridization and band-alignment in AA or AB stacking MoTe2/WSe2. (a), (e), Doping-dependent reflectance contrast spectra at zero electric field (E = 0) near the energy of MoTe2 A1s exciton in (a) AA-stacked and (e) AB-stacked regions. Insets: angle-resolved SHG intensity used to identify the stacking configurations. Electric- field-dependent reflectance contrast near the energy of WSe2 A1s exciton in AA-stacked region at filling factors (b) ν = 0, (c) ν = -0.5, and in AB-stacked region at (f) ν = 0, (g) ν = -0.5. Dashed curves in (b) and (f) are the best fit to the two-level model. Arrows in (c) and (g) marks the hole-doped AP and RP. D, h, Schematic of interlayer and...

FIG. 3. Excitation-energy-dependent SHG intensity and phase difference in MoTe2/WSe2. (a) Upper panel: Experimentally measured SHG intensity of WSe2 (red dots) and the calculated |χ(2)|2 from exciton-state-coupling (ESC) calculations (blue line) as a function of excitation photon energy. Lower panel: Corresponding data for MoTe2. (b) Upper panel: Calculated imaginary part of the dielectric function (ε2) for WSe2 from GW-BSE. Inset: k-space envelope function of the C exciton. Lower panel: Calculated ε2 for MoTe2. Inset: k-space envelope function of the D exciton. (c) Upper and lower panels: GW quasiparticle band structures of WSe2 and MoTe2, respectively. FIG. 4. SHG polarization state manipulation in nearly AA stacked MoTe2/WSe2. (a) Schematics of SHG superposition from WSe2 and MoTe2 with a phase difference Δϕ and twist angle α. (b) The coherent synthesis of 𝐼‖(𝜃)) from two SHG fields of the WSe2 layer (red) and the MoTe2 layer (blue), characterized by angle misalignment 3α and phase difference Δϕ. The filled and empty lobes are contrasted to have opposite SHG fields.
